name: Mirande and Moncassin Baïse Valley Loop
name_original: Mirande et Moncassin – boucle dans la vallée de la Baïse
description: This difficult 40.6-mile touring cycling loop around Mirande and Moncassin offers rolling hills and historic Gascon scenery.
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Mirande traverse the undulating countryside of the Gers department in Southwest France. The region features rolling green hills, expansive vineyards, and quiet country roads, providing varied terrain for cyclists. The Baïse River is a prominent natural feature, offering picturesque riverside cycling opportunities. This landscape provides both recreational paths and more engaging climbs. Best touring cycling routes around Mirande The most popular touring cycling route is Abbaye de Saint-Sever-de-Rustan – loop in the valley of l'Arros, an 11.5 miles (18.5 km) trail that takes 1 hour 13 minutes to complete. This easy route follows the Arros river valley. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Bastides of Bassoues and Marciac Loop, a moderate 19.2 miles (30.8 km) path. This route connects historic bastide towns through the Gascony landscape. Local touring cyclists also love the Mirande and Moncassin Baïse Valley Loop, a 40.6 miles (65.3 km) trail leading through rolling hills and the Baïse River valley, often completed in about 4 hours 3 minutes. Touring cycling around Mirande is defined by rolling green hills, vineyards, and the Baïse River. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult ability levels. The routes in Mirande are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 60 reviews. Nearly 1000 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Mirande's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when touring cycling around Mirande?
A: The Mirande region, often called "France's little Tuscany," features a beautiful, undulating countryside. You'll encounter rolling green hills, expansive vineyards, golden wheat fields, and vibrant sunflower fields. While many routes are recreational, the Gascony landscape's slightly hilly nature provides engaging climbs for more experienced cyclists, offering a diverse experience from quiet country roads to dedicated cycle paths.

Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in the Mirande area?
A: There are over 160 touring cycling routes around Mirande documented on komoot. These routes cater to various skill levels, including 15 easy, 86 moderate, and 62 difficult options, ensuring there's a path for every cyclist.

Q: Are there any family-friendly cycling routes near Mirande?
A: Yes, the region offers several options suitable for families. The Baïse Valley Cycle Route, for instance, follows the Baïse River and passes through charming bastides. Additionally, portions of the Armagnac Greenway provide generally flatter and beginner-friendly paths along converted old railway tracks, perfect for a relaxed family outing.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or attractions I can see along the cycling routes?
A: Mirande and its surroundings are rich in history and natural beauty. You can explore charming bastide towns like Mirande itself, which boasts a 15th-century cathedral and arcaded squares. Other attractions include the remarkably preserved fortified village of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2700350" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bastide de Bassoues&lt;/a&gt;, the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5456217" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Termes d'Armagnac&lt;/a&gt;, and the tranquil &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4814164" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Château de Saint-Blancard&lt;/a&gt;. Many routes also pass by picturesque rivers and lakes, such as Lake Mirande.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Mirande?
A: The Mirande area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 60 reviews. Cyclists often praise the region's diverse terrain, from gentle riverside paths to challenging climbs, and the stunning scenery of rolling hills, vineyards, and historic villages.

Q: Are there any circular cycling routes that allow me to explore the local area?
A: Absolutely! Many routes in the Mirande area are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1021123595/bastides-of-bassoues-and-marciac-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bastides of Bassoues and Marciac Loop&lt;/a&gt; connects historic towns, while the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1021122318/mirande-and-moncassin-baise-valley-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mirande and Moncassin Baïse Valley Loop&lt;/a&gt; takes you through rolling hills and along the Baïse River.

Q: What is the best time of year to go touring cycling in Mirande?
A: The Mirande region is beautiful for cycling during spring and autumn when the weather is mild and the landscapes are vibrant with wildflowers or autumn colors. Summer also offers pleasant conditions, especially in the mornings or late afternoons, with the added beauty of golden wheat and sunflower fields. Electric bike rentals are available to help manage inclines during warmer months.

Q: Are there any longer, more challenging touring cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: Yes, for experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1021123199/lac-de-la-gimone-and-sansan" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lac de la Gimone and Sansan&lt;/a&gt; offer significant distances and elevation gains. This particular route covers over 90 kilometers with substantial climbing, providing a demanding yet rewarding experience through the Gascony landscape.

Q: Can I find quiet roads with minimal traffic for cycling in Mirande?
A: The Gers department is known for its quiet country roads, making it ideal for touring cyclists looking to avoid heavy traffic. The extensive network includes dedicated cycle paths and greenways, ensuring a peaceful and immersive experience through the picturesque countryside.

Q: Are there options for shorter, easier rides for beginners or those looking for a relaxed pace?
A: Certainly! For a more relaxed experience, consider routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1021120656/abbaye-de-saint-sever-de-rustan-loop-in-the-valley-of-l-arros" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Abbaye de Saint-Sever-de-Rustan – loop in the valley of l'Arros&lt;/a&gt;. This easy 18.5 km trail follows the Arros river valley, offering a gentle ride suitable for beginners or those who prefer a leisurely pace.

Q: What kind of wildlife might I encounter while cycling in the Mirande region?
A: As you cycle through the diverse landscapes of Mirande, you might spot various birds, including raptors soaring above the rolling hills. The tranquil forest regions, such as the Forest of Saint-Blancard, and the numerous rivers and lakes also provide habitats for smaller mammals and aquatic life, offering opportunities for wildlife observation.

Q: Are there any routes that follow historical paths or pilgrimage routes?
A: Yes, the renowned Way of St. James (Chemin de Saint-Jacques) extends through the Gers department, offering well-signed paths that can be explored by touring cyclists. These routes often pass through historic villages and provide a unique cultural and scenic experience.
tips: This rather unusual topographic feature must have been the seat of a kind of defensive building. Today, only two crosses remain and the stunning views that can be found at the top.

Bastide in the heart of Gers, holder of the international Cittaslow label dedicated to quality of life. It has a beautiful 15th century cathedral and an interesting earthenware museum. It’s also the perfect place to enjoy a croustade! Just outside the town, there is a very beautiful lake on the shady banks of the Baïse.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this touring cycling loop?
faqs.answer: The terrain around Mirande and Moncassin is characterized by rolling, bucolic hills. You'll primarily ride on small, low-traffic country roads with mostly smooth surfaces. While generally manageable, be prepared for some challenging sections with long and regular climbs, especially if venturing further south from Mirande. Agricultural vehicle use can occasionally lead to muddy or gritty patches.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this touring cycling loop?
faqs.answer: This touring cycling loop is approximately 65 kilometers long and typically takes around 4 hours to complete, depending on your pace and stops.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the Mirande and Moncassin touring cycling loop?
faqs.answer: You can typically find parking in Mirande, a central point for starting this loop. Look for public parking areas within the town, especially near the main square (Place d'Astarac).

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The Baïse Valley is beautiful throughout many seasons. Spring and autumn generally offer pleasant temperatures for cycling. Summers can be warm, but the scenery is vibrant. Always check local weather conditions before you go.

faqs.question: Is this touring cycling loop dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route primarily uses public roads, it's generally possible to bring well-behaved dogs if they are accustomed to cycling alongside or in a trailer. Always ensure your dog is on a leash when off the bike, especially in villages or near livestock, and be mindful of local regulations.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this touring loop in the Baïse Valley.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: You'll experience picturesque and varied scenery, including bucolic hills, typical Gers bastide settlements, orchards, vineyards, grain fields, and forests. The region is often referred to as 'French Tuscany' due to its gentle rolling landscapes.

faqs.question: Are there any notable points of interest on the loop?
faqs.answer: Yes, the loop passes by several interesting spots. You can visit the Viewpoint and Motte Castrale of Moncassin, the Tercentenary oak and chapel, and the charming town of Mirande itself, known for its Gothic church and historic timber-frame houses.

faqs.question: Does this touring cycling loop connect with other trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route overlap with other paths. Notably, about 16% of the loop follows the Véloroute de la Baïse. Smaller portions also intersect with the Sentier de la Digue, Allée Charles de Gaulle, Chemin des Écoliers, and Chemin des Pétunias.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or stay near the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, Mirande offers various amenities, including cafes, restaurants, and accommodation options. As you cycle through the Baïse Valley, you'll find charming towns and villages that may offer additional places for refreshments or overnight stays.

faqs.question: What should I pack for this touring cycling trip?
faqs.answer: Given the rolling hills and potential for varied terrain, we recommend packing appropriate cycling gear, including a helmet, water bottles, snacks, basic repair tools, and layers of clothing. Sun protection is also advisable, especially during warmer months.

faqs.question: Is there a recommended direction to cycle this loop for an easier experience?
faqs.answer: While the overall difficulty is rated as 'difficult' due to elevation changes, some sections of the broader V82 route, which this loop is part of, are described as having 'long and regular climbs'. Consider starting your ride with the more significant ascents if you prefer to tackle them when you're freshest, or research the elevation profile to choose a direction that best suits your climbing preference.

waypoints.name: Viewpoint and Motte Castrale of Moncassin
